evidence_quotes.quote: Submission Policy I get it; sometimes life has a way of upsetting the best laid plans. Since this is probably not your only course this semester, getting behind in your work creates a definite problem. Don't panic! I encourage you to work with me as soon as you encounter a problem meeting a due date. Don't wait! Working together, we can usually create a plan to keep you learning. Key Points Reflections have a built in 5 day grace period for late submission. Quizzes are eligible for late work with prior approval. No exceptions. Late work must still be submitted via the assignment. No work will be accepted via Canvas Inbox or email. Late initial discussion posts are acceptable but will lose 2 points.

Discussion Work Policy Discussions are not eligible for makeup work, as per the late work policy. However, Reflection Assignments based on discussions can and should be done to the best of your ability. Discussions become 'Read Only' after the due date, so you should still be able to review the conversations even if you were unable to participate.

During the semester, the best way to reach me is via Canvas Inbox. I normally check my inbox throughout the day, Monday-Friday. You can expect a response within 24 hours. If you don't hear from me within 24 hours, please resend your message. I am not generally available on weekends or campus holidays.

missing_items: Explicit academic honesty/integrity policy with clear expectations
Formal withdrawal or drop policy explaining process
rubric_findings.suggested_fix: Add a dedicated section on academic honesty/integrity policy with clear definitions and consequences, and include a formal withdrawal/drop policy with relevant campus deadlines and procedures.
rubric_findings.reasoning: The course clearly addresses three of the five required policy areas: work submission (detailed late work policy with specific expectations), communication (Canvas Inbox as primary method with 24-hour response time), and participation (discussion expectations and grading). While academic honesty and withdrawal policies are not explicitly stated in the provided content, the three substantively addressed areas meet the baseline threshold for alignment.

missing_items: No list or explanation of required technology tools found
No instructions for accessing technology (links, downloads, login info)
No technical support information (help desk contact, links)
rubric_findings.suggested_fix: Add a dedicated 'Technology Requirements' page or section in the Welcome module that lists all required tools (Canvas, Zoom, Pronto, TutorMe, etc.), explains their learning purpose, provides access instructions with links, and includes technical support contact information (Student Help Desk, Canvas 24/7 support).
rubric_findings.reasoning: The supplied content is primarily binary/encoded data from what appears to be a Word document (syllabus) and image files. No readable text content is available that would demonstrate identification of required technology, access guidance, or technical support information. Without accessible text content to evaluate, the required evidence for alignment cannot be located.

missing_items: No AI policy, guidance, or substantive AI content found
No instructor disclosure of AI use or stance
No student-centered AI approach with clear usage expectations
rubric_findings.suggested_fix: Add a dedicated AI page or section in the syllabus/orientation module that explains the instructor's stance on AI use, provides clear guidance on when and how students may use AI tools for coursework (e.g., for brainstorming, summarizing, editing), and addresses how the instructor uses or incorporates AI in teaching.
rubric_findings.reasoning: The supplied course content includes the syllabus, welcome materials, assignment instructions, and other course pages. After reviewing all accessible content, no AI-related policy, guidance, or substantive discussion was found. There is no indication of instructor stance on AI use, no course-specific AI approach, and no guidance for students on appropriate AI tool usage.

missing_items: No reference to institutional privacy or security policies
No guidance on safe technology use (protecting credentials, secure connections, video privacy settings)
No mention of privacy considerations for external tools or data handling practices
rubric_findings.suggested_fix: Add a dedicated section in the syllabus or Welcome module that links to the institution's privacy policy and provides explicit guidance on protecting login credentials, using secure connections, and setting video privacy appropriately when using Canvas and any external tools.
rubric_findings.reasoning: The supplied course content consists primarily of binary/encoded data from a syllabus file and various module pages covering course content, assignments, and resources. Despite extensive review of all readable sections including the Welcome module, syllabus references, and multiple unit pages, there is no mention of institutional privacy/security policies, no guidance on safe technology practices, and no discussion of data handling or FERPA. The absence of any privacy or security information means this element cannot be rated as aligned.

missing_items: Links to disability/accessibility services
Links to tutoring services
Links to counseling or student support services
Links to library resources
Links to technology help or help desk
Links to basic needs support
rubric_findings.suggested_fix: Create a dedicated 'Student Resources and Support' page in the Welcome module or syllabus that provides clearly labeled links to at least four of the six required service categories: disability services, counseling, tutoring, library, basic needs, and technology help. Include direct URLs or contact information for each service.
rubric_findings.reasoning: The supplied content consists primarily of binary/encoded data from the syllabus document that is unreadable in its current form. While the course navigation mentions an Academic Success Center, TutorMe, and library resources in instructional contexts, no clearly labeled, easy-to-find links to the required institutional support services (disability services, counseling, tutoring, library, basic needs, technology help) are present in the readable portions of the supplied content. Without evidence of at least four of the six service categories being addressed with actual links or clear references, the element cannot be rated as aligned.

Multiple examples show tips, reminders, helpful links, and tutorials embedded throughout the course content (not just in orientation). rubric_findings.suggested_fix: Already meets baseline requirements; no action needed.
rubric_findings.reasoning: The course demonstrates clear alignment with Element 2.1. Module 1 serves as a clearly labeled 'Welcome to Your Course' starting point with orientation content. The 15 modules show consistent organization with numbered units, descriptive titles, and parallel structure (each starting with 'From the Podium,' followed by overview, activities, and summary). Page 1.2 explicitly provides navigation guidance explaining how to use Canvas. All modules follow the same pattern of overview → materials → activities → summary, demonstrating consistent structure throughout.

evidence_quotes.quote: Identify important course policies. Navigate the online course environment. Build classroom community.

Identify and explain the cultural circumstances that give rise to the oral literature of Native American tribal cultures. Compare the vast philosophic and spiritual differences between colonists and indigenous peoples of the Americas. Apply close reading techniques to identify and analyze key or recurring images and characters in Native American works.

Compare the documented experiences of pre-colonial explorers Cabeza de Vaca and John Smith in order to consider the complex social, cultural, and political contexts for their writings. Compare the philosophic and spiritual differences between Cabeza de Vaca and Smith, and between these explorers and the indigenous peoples they encountered. Identify archetypal elements in the works of Cabeza de Vaca and Smith.

rubric_findings.suggested_fix: None needed. Module objectives are present, consistently placed, use measurable action verbs, and connect to course-level learning outcomes.
rubric_findings.reasoning: Module learning objectives are present in all reviewed modules (Units 1-13). They are consistently placed at the start of each module in the Unit Overview pages. The objectives use measurable action verbs (identify, explain, compare, analyze, apply, discuss) rather than vague terms like 'understand' or 'learn about.' The objectives describe specific, measurable outcomes and connect to what students will do (reading, reviewing, participating in discussions, completing assignments). This meets all baseline requirements for the 'aligned' rating.

evidence_quotes.quote: To meet the unit objectives, you will: Read : Read the "Period Introduction: Beginnings to 1700", pp 3-13 Read the Headnote, "Native American Oral Literature," p 29 The Iroquois Creation Story, p 31 Trickster Tales, pp 35-38 Note: all pages numbers are for the NAAL, Shorter 9th edition, Beginnings to 1865. Review: The contents of this module, including the micro lecture. The associated enrichment resources are optional and designed to provide opportunities for you to futher explore specific topics or authors. Participate : Show What You Know practice quizzes: Period Intro/Native American Oral Literature and the Iroquois Creation Story Discussion of key or recurring images Reflection: Key Characters

Identify and explain the cultural circumstances that give rise to the oral literature of Native American tribal cultures. Compare the vast philosophic and spiritual differences between colonists and indigenous peoples of the Americas. Apply close reading techniques to i dentify and analyze key or recurring images and characters in Native American works.

rubric_findings.suggested_fix: Consider adding explicit statements in module materials that directly connect learning objectives to specific activities and assessments to make the alignment even more transparent to students.
rubric_findings.reasoning: Module 2 shows clear alignment between stated objectives and content/assessments. The objectives focus on identifying cultural circumstances, comparing worldviews, and applying close reading to Native American works. The activities directly support these: readings include cultural/historical context (Period Introduction, Headnotes), primary texts (Iroquois Creation Story, Trickster Tales), quizzes assess knowledge of content, discussions analyze recurring images, and reflections focus on key characters—all matching the stated learning outcomes. This pattern is consistent across multiple modules examined.

evidence_quotes.quote: Each module contains content, activities, and assessments. You are encouraged to read ahead whenever possible.

The most common tasks in the course module include: Accessing course materials, which can include tabbed Canvas content pages, captioned videos, online library articles, open source readings, or general Canvas page content. Community-building, which can include peer reviews, discussions, contributing to a Canvas page, group work, etc. Taking Quizzes and/or exams. Practice quizzes and formal exams always appear in your To Do list. Practice quizzes can be taken multiple times and are untimed. Submitting Assignments, which can be via text-box entry, uploaded submissions (papers, reflections, PowerPoint presentations, images, media files, etc). Assignments can be graded or ungraded.

rubric_findings.suggested_fix: None needed; Canvas tools are used appropriately for organizing, submitting, and interacting.
rubric_findings.reasoning: The course demonstrates intentional use of Canvas tools. Modules organize content sequentially across 13 units. Assignments are used for graded work with clear submission methods. Discussions are used for interaction activities. Resources are embedded within structured Canvas pages rather than scattered files. Evidence shows appropriate tool selection for each activity type, meeting all baseline aligned criteria.

rubric_findings.suggested_fix: Continue integrating multiple media types throughout all modules and ensure all visual content includes alt text for full accessibility compliance.
rubric_findings.reasoning: The course demonstrates consistent use of multiple media types beyond text across modules: captioned videos ('From the Podium' micro lectures in each unit), embedded video tutorials (with CC available), images, and interactive Canvas pages. Captions are explicitly mentioned for videos. Content includes diverse perspectives through readings from Native American, African American, women, and various cultural authors (Wheatley, Murray, Douglass, Jacobs, Davis, Alcott, etc.). While alt text for images is not explicitly documented in the supplied excerpts, the presence of multiple accessible media types and diverse content perspectives satisfies the aligned criteria.

evidence_quotes.quote: During the semester, the best way to reach me is via Canvas Inbox. I normally check my inbox throughout the day, Monday-Friday. You can expect a response within 24 hours. I monitor our weekly discussions daily and will chime in with opinions and clarifications as it seems appropriate. The Community Knowledge Base is where I encourage you to post general questions about the class - and feel free to answer someone's question when you can! ... Every week, I'll record a brand new short video for you where I preview the module content, assigned readings, and share biographical, literary, or historical information to provide context for the work. ... I do my best to grade discussion and reflection assignments and offer feedback within 3-5 days. I will let you know via an announcement if grading is delayed, and provide a new timeline for feedback. ... I use general announcements to let you know when a new module opens, to send assignment reminders, or to share information I think will be valuable for you. ... Each week, I host a drop-in student hour on Thursdays from 4-5pm. Everyone is welcome to ask a question, get assignment clarification, or just discuss the reading.

evidence_quotes.quote:
Instructions
For this assignment, you will create a Journal Article summary, which is not the same as an abstract. Please follow these steps:
Complete the 5 Library Micro-courses [if you haven't already done so].
Locate one academic journal article from either JSTOR or Academic Search Complete, on any author or topic related to slavery and abolitionism.
Read your chosen article and use any active reading strategy to work through the primary points.
Create a summary based on this Journal Article Summary Example. Be sure to include all the necessary information, as seen in the sample, highlighting the author's main argument and primary points. Your goal is to provide an at-a-glance overview of the article for another reader. Please also include the permalink to your article at the end of your summary.

Due Date
This assignment is due by Tuesday at 11:59pm PST.

Submitting this Assignment
All COS students have access to Office 365 via their campus accounts. Office 365 will allow you to create and view Word docs. You are also welcome to use Google Docs. Submit this assignment as a file upload. Submit doc, docx, or pdf files only.

Instructions
Use MLA format: please create a title for your reflection, double space your work, and include in-text citations for any quotes or paraphrases, as well as the full citation for the work(s) you use in a works cited section. 500 words should be sufficient (but you can write more).
Before reading this story, most of us have heard of the character of Rip Van Winkle, and we have heard the phrase "the American Dream" being used in different contexts. Having read the story, how do you think we should understand the character of Rip Van Winkle? For example, is he an example of a Romantic hero or something else? Ultimately, if this story is the first "American Dream" in American literature, what do you think about the implications of that "dream"?
Tip: create a strong thesis statement that is an answer to the questions asked above and build your essay from there.
Note: You are not normally asked to do outside research for these assignments, and you should rely on the assigned readings for your primary evidence as you support your observations and conclusions. If you wish to bring in relevant secondary research in the form of books, scholarly articles, or any of the web resources provided in this module, be sure to cite these sources in MLA format, as well.

Due Date
This reflection is due by Tuesday at 11:59pm PST.

Submitting this Assignment
All COS students have access to Office 365 via their campus accounts. Office 365 will allow you to create and view Word docs. You are also welcome to use Google Docs. Submit this assignment as a file upload. Submit doc, docx, or pdf files only.

evidence_quotes.quote: I do my best to grade discussion and reflection assignments and offer feedback within 3-5 days. I will let you know via an announcement if grading is delayed, and provide a new timeline for feedback.

rubric_findings.suggested_fix: No changes needed. The course clearly establishes feedback timelines and methods across multiple locations.
rubric_findings.reasoning: The course provides clear and consistent information about feedback timelines (3-5 days for discussions/reflections, 3 days for journal articles, within 4 days for exams) and delivery methods (rubric comments, gradebook comments). Students are told where to find feedback (Gradebook) and how to access it. This information appears in multiple accessible locations including assignment pages, the communication plan, and the syllabus area.

missing_items: No self-reflection prompts or assignments asking students to think about their learning process
No learning journals, progress reflections, or self-assessment activities found
No evidence of multiple reflection opportunities distributed throughout the course focused on metacognitive thinking
rubric_findings.suggested_fix: Add explicit self-reflection prompts in multiple modules asking students to reflect on their learning process, such as 'What strategies helped you understand this week's material?' or 'How has your approach to analyzing literature evolved?' Include a mid-course learning journal assignment where students assess their progress toward course objectives.
rubric_findings.reasoning: The supplied content contains numerous reflection assignments throughout the course, but these are analytical essays about literature (e.g., 'Reflection: Rip Van Winkle,' 'Reflection: The Gothic Imagination') rather than metacognitive self-reflections about the learning process itself. There is one 'Muddy Point' assignment asking students to identify unclear concepts, and two 'Check In' surveys, but these do not constitute multiple opportunities for students to reflect on their own learning process and growth. The reflections present are content-focused rather than process-focused.

missing_items: No anonymous or confidential learner feedback survey found in the course content
No feedback mechanism placed in the second half or near the end of the course
No clear invitation for students to provide feedback about course design, pacing, or content
rubric_findings.suggested_fix: Add an anonymous survey or feedback form in the second half of the course (e.g., in a later module or near the final module) that explicitly invites students to provide confidential feedback about course design, pacing, clarity of instructions, and what to keep or change. Include clear privacy assurance language such as 'your responses are anonymous' or 'does not affect your grade.'
rubric_findings.reasoning: The supplied content includes the course syllabus, welcome materials, and 13 units of course content. While there are references to 'Check in with an introduction' and mentions of 'Check-In and Feedback surveys' occurring 'at various times throughout the semester' (page g3e75789416693cf1580adf313395b45c), no actual feedback survey or mechanism is present in the provided content. No specific survey asking students about course design, pacing, workload, or what to keep/change is found. Without evidence of an anonymous/confidential feedback mechanism in the course content, the element cannot be rated as aligned.
